filter_index.video_title: Why We Love The Deoriatal Chandrashila Trek | A Tribute By Indiahikes

temperature_notes: Winter: daytime 8–14 °C, nighttime -6 to -10 °C; 5 warm layers required
Spring: daytime 13–17 °C, nighttime drops to zero; 3–4 warm layers required
Summer: daytime 18–24 °C, nighttime 3–8 °C; 2 warm layers required
Autumn: daytime 10–15 °C, nighttime drops to zero; 3–4 warm layers required

experience_suitability: First-time trekkers as well as experienced trekkers (altitude sickness can happen to anybody)
terrain_challenges: Trekking on the Deoriatal Chandrashila trail in winter can be tricky
Hard frozen snow section from Baniyakund to Chandrashila summit
Snow section getting steeper as you approach the summit
Rain can flood the trails or make them too slushy to walk safely
Snowfall can obstruct previously made paths, requiring fresh tracks to be cut on the snow
altitude_or_weather_risks: Chandrashila summit at 12,083 ft; climb is gradual, making acclimatisation easy
Above 10,000 ft, risk of Acute Mountain Sickness (AMS) is real
AMS can affect first-time and experienced trekkers alike
Even a fit person climbing too high too soon runs equal risk of AMS
Indiahikes team equipped with high-altitude medical kits, oxygen cylinders, and stretchers

fitness_benchmark: Walk, jog or run 5 km in under 35 minutes
This is the minimum recommended fitness level that you must aim for
Train your core, calves, glutes, quadriceps, and hamstrings
approval_requirements: Make sure you upload these screenshots 15 to 20 days before your trek date
Your screenshots must demonstrate that you are meeting the fitness criteria for your trek
Your runs don't meet your trek's fitness criteria at least 3 times in the past month
Your fitness records will be rejected if you haven't uploaded your 3 screenshots to your dashboard
Children below 14 years are not required to submit fitness records
prep_window: Follow this, and you'll be trek-ready in 6 weeks
Runs must meet fitness criteria at least 3 times in the past month

experience_themes: Winter: snow-covered landscape, powdery snow on pine trees, possible snowfall, snowball fights, mighty mountain views

Spring: last of winter snow, receding snow at lower campsites, snow still on upper reaches (Chandrashila, Tungnath) until mid-April, green meadows, blooming tiny flowers, rhododendrons on trees, colour contrasts of snow/greenery/flowers
Summer: bright green forests and meadows, lush flora after early June light showers, easiest trek conditions, escape from plains heat
Autumn: post-monsoon clear skies, washed-clean views, clearest views of the Garhwal Himalayas
Winter: white snow-covered landscape and valley
Spring: colour contrasts of snow, greenery, and flowers on meadows
primary_watchouts: Winter: snow raises trek difficulty by a notch; requires good preparation
July–August: peak monsoon in Uttarakhand; trek not accessible
Travellers planning in July or August due to peak monsoon closure
Trekking on the Deoriatal Chandrashila trail in winter can be tricky
Hard frozen snow section from Baniyakund to Chandrashila summit
Snow section getting steeper as you approach the summit
best_for: Colour contrasts of snow, greenery, and flowers (Mar–Apr)
Easiest trek conditions, lush green landscapes, family/children introduction to Himalayas (May–Jun)
not_ideal_for: Travellers planning in July or August due to peak monsoon closure
